Asia's Powerhouses Urged to Step Up Emission Cuts by Major Investor Group
data.description:
By Jason Chau and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa


Asia's largest electric utilities need to step up their emissions-reduction efforts, particularly as the current conflict in the Middle East underscores the need to think about long-term resilience planning, an investor group says.

"The conflict poses supply shocks, impacting energy prices and it's an active issue that utility companies need to consider both in the short term as a response measure," said a spokesperson for the Asia Investor Group on Climate Change, a network of institutional investors representing $36 trillion in assets under management.

Asia's powerhouses have made notable progress in decarbonization in recent years, but more needs to be done, the group said in a report on Thursday.

"Investors need clear, actionable transition plans from companies that demonstrate how their ambitions will be executed," said Rebecca Mikula-Wright, chief executive of AIGCC.

In an assessment of Asian electric utilities' progress on curbing emissions, the group found that most have a decarbonization strategy and have started to disclose how they are moving away from fossil fuels, but none have detailed capital or operational expenditure dedicated to climate adaptation.

Disclosures are also still inconsistent, and investors want stronger evidence that capital allocation is shifting significantly away from high-emitting assets and aligning with climate targets, the group said.

The progress update is part of AIGCC's Asian Utilities Engagement Program, which has 23 investors with a collective $13 trillion in assets under management. These investors engage with eight key power companies on climate change, including China Resources Power, Chubu Electric, JERA, Tenaga Nasional, and Huaneng Power International.

If climate risk is not properly accounted for or managed, utilities stand to lose billions, AIGCC warned.

Climate hazards already cause material costs of around $6.3 billion annually in asset damage and lost revenue, it said. That figure is expected to rise by 33%, exceeding $8.4 billion by 2050 under a scenario of medium-high global warming.

More broadly, the economic cost of climate inaction under current policies would be quite severe for most economies in Asia, the AIGCC spokesperson said.

Asia's power sector is central to global actions against climate change, as the region accounts for about 80% of the world's operational power-generation capacity. The sector itself is responsible for 54% of the region's total carbon emissions.


Write to Jason Chau at jason.chau@wsj.com and Fabiana Negrin Ochoa at fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 2121ET

NRG Energy, Inc. Announces Appointment of Caroline Golin as Chief Growth and Policy Officer
data.description:
NRG Energy Inc. announced the appointment of Caroline Golin, Ph.D., as Chief Growth and Policy Officer. Dr. Golin brings more than two decades of experience in energy strategy, market development, and product innovation to NRG. In her new role, she will focus on driving the strategic execution of key business initiatives that support reliability and affordability as energy demand grows, including the expansion of data center opportunities and residential virtual power plant (VPP) programs. In this capacity, she will also lead NRG?s Government and Regulatory Affairs functions.

Most recently, Dr. Golin was the Founder and CEO of Envision Energy Advisors, where she worked with stakeholders across the energy value chain to address complex challenges related to grid planning, capital structuring, procurement strategy, and cross-sector partnerships. Prior to founding Envision Energy Advisors, she was Global Head of Energy Market Development and Innovation at Google, where she led energy strategy, new product innovation, and partnership development to support the company?s energy and digital infrastructure needs. She was also a co-creator of Google?s ambitious moonshot program to operate fully on carbon-free energy by 2030.

Dr. Golin holds both a Master?s degree and a Ph.D. from the Georgia Institute of Technology.

Philippines Inflation Sped Up in February
data.description:
By Fabiana Negrin Ochoa and Amanda Lee


Inflation in the Philippines sped up last month, in line with the central bank's expectations and marking the strongest print since January last year.

Price stability is the primary mandate of Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas, and it is likely watching inflation trends particularly closely as the Middle East conflict jolts commodities markets.

In February, the headline consumer-price index rose 2.4% in February from a year earlier, accelerating from January's 2.0% increase, the Philippine Statistics Authority said Thursday.

That compared with the central bank's 2.3% to 3.1% projection, and a Wall Street Journal-compiled forecast of 2.35%.

In its monthly outlook, the BSP said it will continue to monitor domestic and international developments to ensure that monetary policy remains consistent with price stability.

Thursday's data showed that core inflation, which excludes selected food and energy items, edged up to 2.9% in February, versus January's 2.8% reading.

For central banks in Asia, the Iran crisis could complicate policymaking, given the region's heavy reliance on the Middle East for its energy needs.

A significant share of Asia's crude supply comes from the Persian Gulf, ING's Deepali Bhargava said, noting that the Philippines relies on the region for almost 90% of its oil needs.

"Any disruption in the Strait of Hormuz--a critical shipping lane--would restrict supply, potentially causing shortages that slow business activity and put pressure on manufacturing across Asia," she wrote in a recent note.

The economic implications of the conflict will depend a lot on how long it lasts, and policymakers have measures like fiscal buffers they can use to blunt the impact.

Bhargava noted that the Philippines tends to feel a stronger inflation hit than some of its neighbors because retail fuel prices are more market-driven and subsidies are limited.

For now, ING thinks that inflation in Asia will stay largely within central bank targets, but warned of the risk of lasting price shocks coupled with currency depreciation, which could limit the scope for interest-rate cuts.

In the past, the combination of a sharp peso weakness and surging oil prices has turned out to be very inflationary, BSP Gov. Eli Remolona said in an interview last month.

In that context, the BSP would step in and slow down the depreciation of the peso, he said.


Write to Fabiana Negrin Ochoa at fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com and Amanda Lee at amanda.lee@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 2052ET

Asian Equities Rebound as Risk Appetite Improves
data.description:
By Ronnie Harui


Asian equities rebounded Thursday morning following Wednesday's selloff as appetite for risky assets improved on the back of strong U.S. economic data released overnight.

Japan's Nikkei Stock Average rose 3.6%, South Korea's Kospi climbed around 11% and Singapore's FTSE Straits Times Index added 0.8%. Korea Exchange briefly suspended trading in Seoul early Thursday to cool volatility following sharp gains in both the main Kospi and the smaller tech-heavy Kosdaq markets.

The main theme has been risk-on, Commerzbank Research analysts said in a note. "Investors interpreted the positive ISM services index, coupled with a softer price sub-index, as reasons to be optimistic that the U.S. economy could remain resilient amid geopolitical tensions," the analysts said.

U.S. services-sector activity continued to rise in February, according to a monthly survey. The Institute for Supply Management's purchasing managers index for services providers rose to 56.1 in February, higher than economists' expectation of 53.5 in a Wall Street Journal survey.

Some optimism over the progress of the U.S.-Israel military attack on Iran also likely helped to bolster investor sentiment. President Trump said Wednesday that the U.S. military engagement in Iran was doing "very well."

However, oil prices rose on prospects of prolonged supply disruptions driven by the Middle East conflict. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Iran has no hope of outlasting the U.S. in the conflict. He added that the war could last up to eight weeks, though it might be over sooner.

"Oil prices continue to hold up well on the dips amid supply disruption fears in the Middle East," said Fawad Razaqzada, market analyst at FOREX.com.

Front-month West Texas Intermediate crude oil futures were 2.8% higher at $76.75 per barrel, and front-month Brent crude oil futures are were 2.5% higher at $83.46 a barrel.


Write to Ronnie Harui at ronnie.harui@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 2049ET

China sets 2026 growth target at 4.5%-5%, below last year's pace
data.description:
SINGAPORE, March 5 (Reuters) - China has set its 2026 economic growth target at 4.5%-5%, slightly lower than the 5% pace achieved last year, signalling room for greater, albeit not decisive, measures to address industrial overcapacity and rebalance the economy.

Analysts have said a lower growth target gives Beijing more flexibility to implement reforms that make the world's second-largest economy less reliant on exports for growth, having posted a record $1.2 trillion trade surplus in 2025.

COMMENTARY:

MARCO SUN, CHIEF FINANCIAL MARKET ANALYST, MUFG (CHINA), SHANGHAI:

"It appears policymakers intend to maintain a broadly supportive policy stance consistent with the approach taken since 2025. Our onshore desk's quantitative models suggest growth momentum could stabilize and potentially bottom out in 2026. Taken together, these signals imply monetary policy is likely to remain accommodative, with an emphasis on supporting "new economy" sectors, particularly AI and related industries, through lower financing costs and targeted credit support, rather than broad-based stimulus."

MOHAMED EL-ERIAN, CHIEF ECONOMIC ADVISER, ALLIANZ:

"Facing internal and external headwinds, China has dialled back its 2026 growth target to 4.5-5.0%. While this marks the lowest since 1991, it may still prove ambitious unless Beijing aggressively ramps up domestic economic reforms."

LI HAO, RESEARCH DIRECTOR, CYPRESS FUND, BEIJING:

"China set a more proactive fiscal stance than last year, targeting a 4% deficit ratio, alongside moderately loose monetary policy and a continued focus on boosting domestic demand.

"Policymakers unveiled a new tool to spur consumption: a 100 billion yuan 'fiscal-financial coordination' fund dedicated to lifting demand, signalling more targeted support. Funding for science and technology will also increase, with nationwide R&D expenditure planned to grow more than 7% annually, outpacing the GDP growth target.

"The GDP target's lower bound is set at 4.5%, under the market's roughly 5% consensus, underscoring an emphasis on higher-quality growth."

(Reporting by Reuters Asia bureaus; Compiled and edited by Sherry Jacob-Phillips)

data.title: Anthropic chief back in talks with Pentagon about AI deal - FT
PRECIOUS - Gold gains on safe-haven demand, softer dollar
data.description:
March 5 (Reuters) - Gold rose on Thursday as the widening Middle East conflict drove investors towards the safe-haven asset, while a softer dollar also lent support to bullion prices.

FUNDAMENTALS

* Spot gold was up 0.8% at $5,176.69 per ounce by 0101 GMT. U.S. gold futures for April delivery were up 1% at $5,186.30.

* The U.S. dollar edged lower, making greenback-priced gold less expensive for holders of other currencies. [USD/]

* The U.S.-Iran war widened sharply on Wednesday after a U.S. submarine sank an Iranian warship off Sri Lanka, killing at least 80 people, and NATO air defences destroyed an Iranian ballistic missile fired towards Turkey.

* The escalation came as the powerful son of Iran's slain supreme leader emerged as a frontrunner to succeed him, suggesting Tehran was not about to buckle to pressure, five days after the U.S. and Israel launched a military campaign that has killed hundreds and convulsed global markets.

* Bullion, traditionally viewed as a safe-haven asset, has risen about 20% so far this year, notching successive record highs amid heightened global political and economic uncertainty.

* U.S. President Donald Trump on Wednesday officially nominated former Federal Reserve Governor Kevin Warsh to be the U.S. central bank's next chair, the White House said, putting the president one step closer to installing a rate-cut-friendly Fed chief.

* Markets expect the Fed to keep interest rates steady at the conclusion of its two-day meeting on March 18, according to CME Group's FedWatch tool.

* Investors now await the weekly U.S. jobless claims data due later in the day and the U.S. employment report for February on Friday.

* Spot silver rose 1.2% to $84.43 per ounce. Spot platinum added 2.1% to $2,193.65, while palladium rose 0.5% to $1,683.

DATA/EVENTS (GMT)

0930 UK S&P GLOBAL PMI: MSC COMPOSITE - OUTPUT Feb

1330 US Import Prices YY Jan

1330 US Initial Jobless Clm 28 Feb, w/e

(Reporting by Noel John and Anushree Mukherjee in Bengaluru; Editing by Subhranshu Sahu)

China's New GDP Target Signals Pivot to Quality Over Quantity
data.description:
China set its 2026 economic growth target at between 4.5% and 5%, a widely expected move that acknowledges slowing growth while giving policymakers more flexibility to address weakness at home and uncertainty abroad.

The new goal, announced by Chinese Premier Li Qiang on Thursday, compares with the "around 5%" growth that officials have aimed for over the past three years.

In 2025, China managed to hit that target, as resilient exports buoyed the world's second-largest economy despite a trade war with the U.S.

To help the economy meet expectations this year, the Chinese government said it would keep its fiscal deficit to 4.0% of gross domestic product, the premier said at the opening of the country's annual legislative meeting.

Beijing also set a consumer inflation target of around 2.0% for this year, signaling an effort to avert a deflationary spiral.

Last year, the consumer price index was flat from a year earlier, falling far short of the 2.0% target as the economy grappled with lingering deflationary pressure and weak demand.

Priority was also placed on steadying the labor market, which has been a further drag on consumer sentiment.

In 2026, the government said it plans to create 12 million jobs, same as last year, while capping the headline unemployment rate at 5.5%, also unchanged from 2025.

Local governments in China will be allowed to issue a total of 4.4 trillion yuan or about $637.91 billion in special-purpose bonds, which are normally used to fund investment projects, underlining efforts to support growth with fiscal stimulus.

Beijing also announced plans to issue 1.3 trillion yuan worth of ultralong treasury bonds, same as last year's quota.


Write to Singapore editors at singaporeeditors@dowjones.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

03-04-26 2027ET
